questions.question: Define process management and explain its importance in modern operating systems.
keyPoints: Process management handles creation, scheduling, termination of processes
Ensures efficient CPU utilization
Enables multiprogramming and multitasking
Manages process states and resources

questions.question: What is a Process Control Block (PCB)? List its main components.
keyPoints: PCB is a data structure storing process information
Contains: process state, program counter, CPU registers
Memory management info, I/O status, accounting data
Each process has its own PCB maintained by OS

questions.question: Explain the five basic process states with a state transition diagram.
keyPoints: States: New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated
New: Process being created
Ready: Waiting for CPU in ready queue
Running: Currently executing on CPU
Waiting: Blocked for I/O or event
Terminated: Completed execution

questions.question: Differentiate between a program and a process with examples.
keyPoints: Program is passive (static instructions on disk)
Process is active (program in execution)
Multiple processes can run same program
Process has resources: memory, registers, I/O devices

questions.question: What is context switching? Explain its significance in process management.
keyPoints: Context switching saves state of running process
Restores state of another ready process
Involves saving registers, program counter, stack pointer
Enables multiprogramming and CPU sharing

questions.question: Explain the role of short-term, long-term, and medium-term schedulers.
keyPoints: Long-term: Controls degree of multiprogramming, admits processes
Short-term: Selects which ready process gets CPU (CPU scheduler)
Medium-term: Handles process swapping between memory and disk
Each operates at different time scales and purposes

questions.question: What is the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling?
keyPoints: Preemptive: OS can forcibly take CPU from running process
Non-preemptive: Process runs until it yields or terminates
Preemptive provides better response time and fairness
Preemptive requires careful synchronization

questions.question: Describe the complete lifecycle of a process from creation to termination. Include all state transitions and system calls involved.
keyPoints: Process creation via fork()/exec() or CreateProcess()
New state -> Ready state (admission by long-term scheduler)
Ready -> Running (dispatched by short-term scheduler)
Running -> Waiting (I/O request or wait system call)
Waiting -> Ready (I/O completion or event occurs)
Running -> Ready (time slice expires or preemption)
Running -> Terminated (exit system call or fatal error)
OS reclaims resources and removes PCB

questions.question: Explain the concept of inter-process communication (IPC) and discuss its importance in process management. Mention common IPC mechanisms.
keyPoints: IPC allows processes to communicate and synchronize
Shared memory: Processes share a common memory region
Message passing: Processes exchange messages via OS
Pipes, sockets, semaphores for synchronization
Essential for cooperation between processes
Prevents race conditions and ensures data consistency

questions.question: Discuss the various process scheduling criteria that operating systems must consider. How do different scheduling algorithms prioritize these criteria?
keyPoints: CPU Utilization: Keep CPU as busy as possible
Throughput: Number of processes completed per unit time
Turnaround Time: Time from submission to completion
Waiting Time: Time spent in ready queue
Response Time: Time from request to first response
Algorithms: FCFS (fair but poor turnaround), SJF (optimal turnaround but starve), Priority (important first but may starve), Round Robin (good response time)

questions.question: What are daemon processes and zombie processes? Explain how they are created and managed in Unix/Linux systems.
keyPoints: Daemon: Background process without controlling terminal
Created by forking child and having parent call wait() immediately
Common daemons: init, httpd, sshd, cron
Zombie: Terminated process whose parent hasn't read exit status
Parent uses wait()/waitpid() to read status
If parent dies, init inherits and reaps zombie
Zombies consume minimal process table entries
